nativeRange.habitat: Protected upper reef slopes and fore-reef slopes in clear, tropical waters; commonly collected from the Great Barrier Reef and Indo-Pacific reefs

decorPreferences: open area with spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding of small meaty foods — mysis shrimp, chopped krill, brine shrimp, or LPS pellet/powder mixes — is recommended once per week. Feed during evening hours when tentacles are extended; turning off flow during feeding improves uptake. Overfeeding has been reported to cause tissue deformation.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS such as Galaxea or Hammer without spacing
bulldozing organisms such as large hermit crabs or urchins that may damage tissue

imageCandidates.notes: Robert Howie, 3994x2755. Aquarium colony under blue/actinic reef lighting showing vivid fluorescent orange fleshy lobes with teal-green valleys — exactly the hobbyist Lobophyllia look; sharp, whole colony framed.
Diego Delso, 6831x4554. L. hemprichii — vivid red fleshy meandering lobed colony, whole colony in frame, tack sharp, in-situ (Anilao, Philippines).
Bernard DUPONT, 3578x2146. Lobophyllia sp. — vivid orange whole colony in-situ on the reef; sharp, shows the fleshy lobed brain-coral form well.
Poco a poco, 4930x4930. L. erythraea — striking red tissue with green-tipped polyps and lavender highlights; sharp Red Sea in-situ view (tissue-surface crop, not whole colony).
Michelle Jonker, 1382x1379. L. hataii — whole colony in-situ with green valleys against brown lobes; sharp, shows the meandering fleshy ridges.
Debra Baker, 1585x1189. L. ishigakiensis — yellow-green whole colony in-situ; adds another distinct species to the genus gallery.
Diego Delso, 4750x4750. L. corymbosa — colorful yellow-and-purple fleshy polyps, sharp Red Sea in-situ view (tissue-surface crop).
Bondolo, 2720x2040. Lobophyllia sp. — sharp top-down aquarium view of a single fleshy polyp with cream ridges and a teal oral disc; clean whole-polyp framing.
Michelle Jonker, 2816x2112. L. flabelliformis — whole colony in-situ; muted gray-green colour but sharp and adds another distinct species to the genus gallery.

summary: Lobophyllia Coral is a genus of fleshy brain corals belonging to the Large Polyp Stony (LPS) group, prized in reef aquariums for vivid coloration and generally considered intermediate in care difficulty. It tends to do well under moderate lighting and low to moderate flow, and benefits from occasional target feeding.
careNotes:
Lobophyllia — commonly called lobo coral or lobed brain coral — is a genus of Large Polyp Stony (LPS) corals belonging to the family Lobophylliidae. Some older references place it in the family Mussidae; Lobophylliidae is the current accepted classification.

Trade name note: The trade names 'Lord coral' and 'Aussie Lord' typically refer to Acanthastrea lordhowensis, a species now reclassified as Micromussa lordhowensis and belonging to a separate genus. Lobophyllia and Acanthastrea/Micromussa are sometimes mixed up in the trade, though they are distinct genera with broadly similar care requirements. Shoppers encountering specimens labeled 'Lord coral' from Australian sources may want to confirm the genus before purchase.

Lighting: Lobophyllia is generally advised to receive moderate light, approximately 75–150 PAR. Hobbyist guidance on the exact range varies — some sources suggest 50–100 PAR to prevent bleaching, while others consider up to 200 PAR acceptable in acclimated specimens. New specimens are best introduced at the lower end of the range and moved incrementally toward brighter positions if desired. Intense SPS-level lighting is broadly considered too strong.

Flow: Low to moderate water movement is generally recommended. Direct, laminar flow tends to cause tissue retraction and may lead to recession over time; indirect, randomized flow at roughly 5–15× tank volume per hour tends to work well. Flow should be strong enough to clear detritus from the coral's surface without stressing the tissue.

Placement: Lobophyllia is best positioned on the sandbed or lowest rockwork, with at least 10–15 cm (4–6 inches) of clear space around the colony on all sides. The coral tends to extend mesenteric filaments (thin digestive threads used offensively) at night to attack neighboring corals; these filaments retract by morning and can easily go unnoticed. Physical sweeper tentacles have been reported to reach up to 12 cm (approximately 5 inches) in some specimens.

Feeding: Lobophyllia is widely regarded as benefiting meaningfully from regular target feeding. Weekly feedings of mysis shrimp, chopped krill, brine shrimp, or commercial LPS foods are commonly recommended, with some sources suggesting every two weeks to avoid tissue distension from overfeeding; weekly is a generally accepted middle ground. Feeding is best done in the evening when tentacles are extended, with flow pumps turned off for 15–30 minutes to allow the coral to take in food.

Water quality: Stable parameters are more important than hitting exact targets. Elevated nitrate (above 30–40 ppm) and elevated phosphate tend to cause tissue stress, while very low-nutrient conditions can also be detrimental. Moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–15 ppm, phosphate 0.05–0.1 ppm) are broadly associated with good coloration and growth. Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium supplementation through a two-part system or calcium reactor is recommended to support ongoing skeletal growth.

Growth: Lobophyllia tends to be a slow grower in captivity. Wild colonies can extend several meters across; aquarium colonies typically reach 20–40 cm over years of growth.

Fragmentation: Lobophyllia can be fragmented, but recovery tends to be slow and the risk of tissue necrosis is higher than with faster-growing LPS corals. Fragging is not commonly attempted in the hobby.
breedingNotes: Lobophyllia reproduces sexually on natural reefs through broadcast spawning, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column. Captive sexual reproduction is not practiced in the hobby. Fragmentation — physically cutting the colony into pieces — is technically possible, but Lobophyllia tends to heal slowly and carries a higher risk of tissue necrosis compared to many other LPS corals, so it is not commonly attempted.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source. Listed scientific name Lobophyllia hemprichii; care level 'Easy'; temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C); SG 1.023–1.025; pH 8.1–8.4; dKH 8–12; lighting moderate-to-high; flow medium; placement all areas; family listed as Mussidae (now superseded by Lobophylliidae).

additional.name: ReefChasers – Lobophyllia Care Guide
additional.notes: Secondary source. PAR 150–200 (moderate); temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C); pH 8.0–8.4; dKH 8–9; Ca 400–450 ppm; care level 'Advanced'; flow moderate; placement sandbed/lower; sweeper tentacle reach up to 5 inches (~12.7 cm); feeding 2–3× weekly mysis/brine. Care level averaged to 'intermediate' across all sources.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Lobophyllia Coral Care
additional.notes: Secondary source. PAR 30–150; dKH 8–9; Ca ~425 ppm; Mg ~1350 ppm; flow low-moderate; placement lower/sandbed; care level intermediate; native range Pacific/Great Barrier Reef. Confirmed nocturnal mesenteric filament aggression.

additional.name: TFH Magazine – Lobophyllia hemprichii
additional.notes: Cross-verification source (TFH is a whitelist secondary for coral-saltwater). Temperature 75–83°F (24–28°C); SG 1.024–1.026; pH 8.1–8.2; Ca 380–430 ppm; Mg ~1272 ppm; dKH 3.5 meq/l (~9.8 dKH); care level beginner/easy; native range Indo-West Pacific and Red Sea including Australia east to Tahiti, north to Japan. Feeding frequency 'no more than once every few weeks' — less frequent than hobbyist consensus; weekly adopted as a safer middle ground. Family listed as Mussidae (older classification; Lobophylliidae is current).

additional.name: Wikipedia – Lobophyllia (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only. Confirmed Family Lobophylliidae, Order Scleractinia. Common names: lobed brain coral, lobo coral. Article is a stub with no husbandry data.
